text: The surgeon pulls the stomach upward to expose the gastrocolic omentum, facilitating entry into the lesser sac.
The surgeon incises the gastrocolic omentum, cutting through the first two layers of the greater omentum to gain access into the lesser sac.
The surgeon cuts through the first two layers of the gastrocolic omentum, revealing the underlying structures and gaining access to the lesser sac.
The surgeon lifts the stomach to expose the third and fourth layers of the greater omentum, which are then incised to gain access to the pancreas within the lesser sac.
The surgeon exposes the lesion located in the body and tail of the pancreas, with the overlying fascia identified as the third layer of the greater omentum, which has been incised.
The surgeon mobilizes the stomach, lifting it to facilitate access to the underlying structures, including the pancreas.
The surgeon uses traction to hitch the stomach up onto the anterior abdominal wall, mobilizing it up to the gastro-duodenal artery, indicated by a red arrow.
The gastro-duodenal artery is visible running along the superior surface of the pancreas, highlighted in the surgical field.
After mobilizing the stomach, the entire pancreas becomes visible, allowing for further dissection.
The surgeon cuts the root of the mesentery of the transverse colon, exposing the inferior border of the pancreas for further dissection.
The surgeon incises the root of the mesentery of the transverse colon, extending from the second part of the duodenum to the tail of the pancreas, exposing the inferior border of the pancreas.
The surgeon completes the incision along the root of the mesentery, extending from the inferior border of the pancreas to the tail, ensuring maximal exposure of the pancreatic region.

The surgeon performs a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to lift the inferior surface and border of the pancreas from the underlying fascia and duodenum, revealing a relatively avascular plane.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection in the avascular zone, easily separating the tissue planes around the pancreas.
The surgeon assesses the splenic vein and splenic artery to determine if they are free from surrounding tissues, following the exposure of the inferior border of the pancreas.
The surgeon hitches the stomach to the anterior abdominal wall, visibly securing it to minimize trauma and keep it away from the dissection area.

The surgeon cuts the root of the mesentery and continues the dissection distally towards the tail of the pancreas, ensuring the entire inferior surface of the pancreas is lifted off from the underlying fascia covering the duodenum and Gerota's fascia.
The surgeon carefully uses a gauze piece to protect the tumor, ensuring no trauma occurs during the dissection process.
The surgeon lifts the undersurface of the normal pancreas away from the Gerota's fascia, revealing the separation between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon assesses the splenic artery and splenic vein to determine if they can be separated, aiming to preserve the spleen during the pancreatectomy procedure.
The tumor, measuring approximately five to six centimeters, is visually observed in close proximity to the splenic artery and splenic vein, without engulfing them.
The surgeon carefully cuts the lower polar division of the splenic artery, which is visually identified as having an early take-off, as previously observed on CT angiography.
The surgeon identifies the splenic vein and splenic artery from the inferior surface of the pancreas, taking care to minimize handling of the tumor during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the splenic artery from the superior border of the pancreas, revealing the main splenic artery running along the superior border.
The surgeon identifies the splenic artery, loops it, and ligates it using hemolog clips, ensuring secure closure of the vessel.
The surgeon uses a vascular loop to cut the remaining attachments of the splenic artery to the pancreas, fully separating the artery from the pancreatic tissue.
After ligating and cutting the splenic artery, the surgeon mobilizes the splenic vein, with the surgical field appearing relatively avascular.

The surgeon uses a gauze piece to retract the tumor, ensuring that laparoscopic instruments do not perforate it. The splenic vein is mobilized to create space for the vascular staplers, which will be used for its ligation.
The surgeon uses a curved tip laparoscopic stapler from Johnson and Johnson to staple the splenic vein, demonstrating the tool's maneuverability in the surgical field.
The surgeon lifts the pancreas to separate its inferior surface from the Gerota's fascia after the splenic artery and vein have been ligated and cut.
The surgeon gently lifts the pancreatic parenchyma, ensuring the tumor is not perforated, using a gauze piece for traction on the normal pancreas.

The surgeon lifts the entire pancreas, cutting the superior attachments along the stomach and pancreas up to the spleen. After severing both superior and inferior attachments, the splenocolic ligament and gastrosplenic short gastric vessels are cut, preparing the pancreatic parenchyma for division using a stapling technique.

The surgeon applies compression to the pancreatic parenchyma for ten minutes before firing the stapler, dividing half of the parenchyma. The remaining half is compressed again for ten minutes before stapling, ensuring complete and safe division of the pancreatic parenchyma with a clear margin.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the pancreas and spleen, ensuring full dissection as part of the pancreatic splenectomy procedure.
